title: Thawing sub‐arctic permafrost: Effects on vegetation and methane emissions
body: Ecosystems along the 0°C mean annual isotherm are arguably among the most sensitive to changing climate and mires in these regions emit significant amounts of the important greenhouse gas methane (CH 4 ) to the atmosphere. These CH 4 emissions are intimately related to temperature and hydrology, and alterations in permafrost coverage, which affect both of those, could have dramatic impacts on the emissions. Using a variety of data and information sources from the same region in subarctic Sweden we show that mire ecosystems are subject to dramatic recent changes in the distribution of permafrost and vegetation. These changes are most likely caused by a warming, which has been observed during recent decades. A detailed study of one mire show that the permafrost and vegetation changes have been associated with increases in landscape scale CH 4 emissions in the range of 22–66% over the period 1970 to 2000. Mire Permafrost Environmental science Subarctic climate Vegetation (pathology) Greenhouse gas Climate change Ecosystem Methane Global warming Peat Physical geography Arctic Atmospheric sciences Atmosphere (unit) Climatology Hydrology (agriculture) Ecology Geology Oceanography Geography Meteorology Biology Pathology Medicine Geotechnical engineering
